  • Google Chrome (x64) – Versions 147.0.7727.101 / 147.0.7727.102

Post validation, confirmed that all patches have been successfully applied and no high vulnerabilities remain. All systems are now compliant as per patch management policy.

Title: Activity log needed
Notes:
Following your request regarding the investigation into the possible unauthorized copying or accessing of files on your system, I wanted to provide a comprehensive update on the actions we have taken and the findings from our investigation.

What We Did:

To investigate the potential file access and movement, we reviewed the event logs on the system and specifically focused on file access and external media activity. We enabled the necessary Audit File System policies to capture future file access events and external data transfers.

We also used PowerShell commands to filter and collect the logs associated with file access and data transfers, ensuring we were capturing all data regarding these activities.

What We Found:

Unfortunately, during our review of the event logs, we did not find any relevant entries for file access or data movement. Upon further inspection, we determined that no logs were generated because the Audit File System policy was not enabled at the time.

Why No Logs Were Recorded:

The root cause of the missing logs was the absence of Object Access Auditing, specifically the Audit File System policy. This policy is essential for tracking access to files, folders, and external media transfers. Without this policy, no logs were created for file access or data transfers to external devices like USB drives.

Next Steps:

To ensure full auditing and visibility moving forward:

  1. We have enabled the required auditing policies for file system access and external media transfers.
  2. Any future file access or data transfers will be logged, providing full visibility and enabling easier tracking of such activities.

Conclusion:

While no logs were recorded due to the lack of Audit File System policy previously, we have now resolved the issue and configured the system to ensure future file access or data movement activities will be properly logged and available for review.

For the  follow-up question regarding the Audit File System policy.

The Audit File System policy is not automatically enabled by default in most environments, as it can generate a high volume of log data, which some organizations may prefer to avoid unless they have specific compliance or security requirements in place. Many businesses enable this policy when there is a known need for detailed tracking of file access and data movement, especially in environments where sensitive data is handled.

In this particular case, it appears that the Audit File System policy was not enabled when the system was initially set up, which is why no file access or external device activity logs were recorded.

Moving forward, we have now enabled the Audit File System policy to ensure that future file accesses, deletions, or transfers to external drives are properly logged, providing better visibility and control over data movement.
